turns.q: I'm brand new to gardening and feeling a bit overwhelmed. Where do I even start?
turns.reply:
Welcome to gardening! It is completely normal to feel overwhelmed at first, but looking at your Maple Cottage Garden, you actually already have a fantastic foundation set up. 

Here is how to ease into it without it taking over your busy weekends:
- **Start small:** You don't have to tend to every single bed perfectly. Focus on just one or two areas first, like your Veg Patch or the Greenhouse.
- **Focus on favorites:** You mentioned you love growing tomatoes, and you already have some planted! Mastering one crop you really enjoy is the best way to learn the ropes.
- **Embrace the cottage style:** You prefer a relaxed, informal look that brings in pollinators. Your Lavender and Sunflowers are perfect for this—they draw in the bees and thrive without needing constant fussing.
- **Ditch the lawn guilt:** You hate mowing, and the good news is that a cottage garden style is the perfect excuse to gradually replace grass with more low-maintenance borders and paths.
- **Just observe:** The best thing a beginner can do is simply look at their plants. Spend 5 minutes a day just walking around with a cup of tea to see how the soil feels and how the leaves look.

→ Would you like me to set up a simple "Garden Walkabout" reminder for your weekends to help you keep an eye on things without it feeling like a chore?

turns.q: I only have a small balcony that gets afternoon sun — what's easy to grow there?
turns.reply:
A sunny balcony is perfect for growing a whole range of colourful, low-maintenance plants in pots.

Here are some easy-to-grow options that will thrive in afternoon sun:
- **Herbs:** Mediterranean herbs love heat and don't mind if you occasionally forget to water. Basil, rosemary, thyme, and sage are all fantastic choices that do well in containers.
- **Fruiting Vegetables:** Compact or "patio" varieties of cherry tomatoes and chilli peppers will produce lots of fruit in a sunny spot. Look for bush types (determinate) rather than vining ones.
- **Flowers:** For a splash of colour, you can't go wrong with pelargoniums (geraniums), marigolds, or petunias. They are very forgiving and will flower all summer long.
- **Leafy Greens:** A "cut-and-come-again" lettuce mix can work well, but may need some shade during the most intense part of the day to prevent it from bolting (flowering).

A key tip for balcony pots is that they dry out faster than garden soil, so on very hot days, they'll likely need a good watering each evening.

→ Shall I add any of these to a shopping list for you?

turns.q: How often should I water a peace lily?
turns.reply:
Peace lilies prefer consistently moist soil, so it's best to water them when the top inch of soil feels dry to the touch.

- **The Droop Test:** This plant is famously dramatic and will droop its leaves noticeably when it's thirsty. It's often better to wait for a slight droop than to overwater.
- **How to Water:** Water thoroughly until it runs out of the drainage holes at the bottom of the pot. Tip out any excess water from the saucer so the plant isn't left sitting in it.
- **Frequency:** This might be roughly once a week in the growing season (spring and summer) and less often in winter, but always check the soil first rather than sticking to a rigid schedule.
- **Water Quality:** Peace lilies can be sensitive to chemicals in tap water. If you notice brown leaf tips, try using filtered, distilled, or rainwater.

turns.q: My tomato leaves are turning yellow — what's wrong?
turns.reply:
Yellowing tomato leaves usually point to a watering imbalance or a nutrient deficiency, though it can also just be the natural aging of the lowest leaves.

- **Watering:** Check the soil a couple of inches down — if it's soggy, hold off on watering so the roots can breathe; if it's bone dry, give it a deep soak.
- **Nutrients:** If the older, lower leaves are yellowing evenly, the plant might need a nitrogen boost (though I see you already have a feeding schedule running, which is great!).
- **Natural aging:** If it's only the very bottom leaves and the rest of the plant is vibrant green, it's completely normal. Just snip them off to improve airflow.
- **Watch for:** Dark spots with yellow halos (a sign of blight) or tiny bugs under the leaves.

→ Want me to set a quick reminder for tomorrow to check the soil moisture and prune off those yellow leaves?

turns.q: How do I fix it?
turns.reply:
Of course. Here are the steps to fix the most common causes of yellowing tomato leaves:

- **For watering issues:** Water deeply at the base of the plant until the soil is thoroughly moist, but then let the top inch or two dry out completely before watering again. This combination of deep watering and a dry-out period is key.
- **For nutrient issues:** Give your tomatoes a dose of a balanced liquid fertilizer rich in nitrogen. A liquid seaweed or specific tomato feed works well. Apply it to the soil around the base, not on the leaves.
- **For all cases:** Snip off any yellowed or spotted leaves with clean scissors or secateurs, cutting close to the main stem. This improves airflow, focuses the plant's energy on producing fruit, and removes any potential disease hosts.

turns.q: We have a crab apple tree, when should we harvest the apples?
turns.reply:
Crab apples are generally ready to harvest from late summer into autumn, typically between August and October, depending on the variety.

The best way to tell if they're ripe is to check for a combination of signs:
- **Colour:** The fruit should have reached its full, vibrant colour (e.g., deep red, bright orange, or golden yellow).
- **Feel:** They should feel firm and crisp when you gently squeeze one.
- **Ease of picking:** A ripe crab apple will detach easily from the branch with a slight upward twist. If you have to pull hard, it's not ready.
- **Pips:** Cut one open. The seeds (pips) inside should be dark brown. If they're pale or white, the fruit needs more time.
- **Taste:** They will still be very tart compared to eating apples, but the flavour should be sharp and fruity, not unpleasantly astringent. They are perfect for making jellies and preserves at this stage.

turns.q: How do I know when my strawberries are ripe?
turns.reply:
Your strawberries are ripe when they are bright red all over, plump, and have a strong, sweet smell.

- **Colour:** Look for a deep, even red right up to the leafy cap. Any white or green areas, especially on the "shoulders" near the stem, mean it's not quite ready.
- **Scent:** A ripe strawberry is very fragrant. If you get close and can't smell anything, it probably won't have much flavour yet.
- **Feel:** It should feel firm and plump. If it's mushy, it's overripe; if it's very hard, it needs more time.
- **Ease of picking:** The berry should come away from the plant with only a very gentle pull. If you have to tug hard, it's not ready.

→ Strawberries ripen quickly once they start. Want me to set up a daily reminder to check on them for the next couple of weeks?

rating.toolNote: Pure harvest-knowledge question correctly answered with no tools.
rating.highlight: Multi-sense ripeness guide (colour, scent, feel, pick-resistance) with a well-judged single offer of a daily check reminder.

turns.q: What's an easy houseplant that's really hard to kill?
turns.reply:
A classic choice is the Snake Plant (*Dracaena trifasciata*)—it's famous for being tough.

*   **Light:** It's happy in low light but thrives in bright, indirect sun.
*   **Watering:** This is key: let the soil dry out completely between waterings. It's far more likely to be harmed by too much water than too little.
*   **Why it's easy:** It tolerates neglect, purifies the air, and handles inconsistent watering like a champ.

turns.q: Do I need to prune my lavender? I've never done it before.
turns.reply:
Yes, it's a good idea to prune lavender every year. Pruning stops the plant from becoming woody and leggy, which encourages lots of fresh, flower-producing growth and extends its life.

- **When:** The best time is in late summer, right after the main flush of flowers has faded.
- **How:** Cut back the flowering stems and about a third of the green, leafy growth. The most important rule is to **never cut back into the old, bare wood** at the base of the plant, as it won't regrow from there.
- **Goal:** Aim to keep a compact, rounded mound shape.

→ I see you have lavender in Raised Bed B. Want me to set a pruning reminder for late August?

turns.q: What should I feed my tomatoes and how often?
turns.reply:
Tomatoes are heavy feeders that need a balanced feed to start, then one high in potassium once they begin to flower.

*   **Before Flowering:** Use a general-purpose balanced liquid feed to encourage strong root and leaf growth.
*   **After Flowering:** Switch to a high-potassium (high 'K') liquid tomato feed. This is crucial for developing lots of healthy fruit. Too much nitrogen at this stage will give you lots of leaves but not many tomatoes.
*   **Frequency:** For plants in beds, feed every 1-2 weeks. If they're in containers, feed weekly as nutrients wash out more quickly.
*   **How to Apply:** Water the feed onto the soil around the base of the plant, trying to avoid splashing the leaves.

→ I see you have a "Feed Tomatoes & Fruit" schedule running every 14 days. Would you like to adjust its frequency or description based on this?

turns.q: I want to grow some herbs together — which ones go well side by side?
turns.reply:
A great rule of thumb is to plant herbs together that have similar needs for sun and water.

*   **Sun-Lovers (Drier Soil):** These Mediterranean herbs thrive in full sun and well-drained soil, and don't need much water. They make a classic pairing.
    - Rosemary, Sage, Thyme, Lavender, Oregano, Marjoram.

*   **Moisture-Lovers (Richer Soil):** These herbs prefer more regular watering and richer soil conditions.
    - Parsley, Basil, Chives, Cilantro.

*   **Keep Separate:** Mint is notoriously invasive and its roots will quickly take over a bed. It's best planted on its own in a container. I notice you have some unplanted mint in your Shed, so a pot would be perfect for it.

*   **Pest Control:** Many herbs are great pest deterrents. Basil is famously planted alongside tomatoes to help repel tomato hornworms and whiteflies.

turns.q: How much sun does a tomato plant actually need?
turns.reply:
Tomato plants need at least 6 to 8 hours of direct sunlight per day to thrive.

- **Full sun is key:** They are fruiting plants and use a lot of energy to produce tomatoes. Abundant, direct sun fuels that growth and fruit development.
- **Too little sun:** Less than 6 hours can lead to tall, spindly ("leggy") stems with sparse leaves, fewer flowers, and a much smaller harvest.
- **In very hot climates:** While they love sun, intense afternoon sun in peak summer can sometimes stress the plants or cause sunscald on the fruit. In those conditions, a little afternoon shade can be helpful.

rating.toolNote: General sunlight fact answered from knowledge, correctly with no tools.

turns.q: I'm going away for two weeks in summer — what do I do about watering?
turns.reply:
You have a few options for keeping your garden watered while you're away, ranging from simple preparation to full automation.

Here are the most common approaches:
*   **Prepare the Garden:** Give everything a deep, thorough soaking the day before you leave. Add a thick layer of mulch (like compost or bark chips) to all your beds to help the soil retain moisture.
*   **Move Pots:** Group all your potted plants together in a shady, sheltered spot. This reduces evaporation and creates a more humid microclimate for them. For special plants, you can stand them in trays of water or use self-watering spikes.
*   **Ask a Friend:** A reliable friend or neighbour is often the simplest solution!
*   **Automated Watering:** The most reliable option is an automated system. Rhozly can connect to smart water valves and soil moisture sensors to water only when needed. You can set up an automation that says, "If the soil moisture in the Veg Patch drops below 35%, open the valve for 20 minutes."

The automated approach is the only one that can react to a sudden heatwave while you're gone.

turns.q: Are coffee grounds actually good for my plants?
turns.reply:
Yes, they can be beneficial for your plants, but it's generally best to compost them first.

Used coffee grounds are a good source of nitrogen and can improve soil structure as they break down. However, using them fresh in large amounts has a few risks:

- **Acidity:** Fresh grounds are acidic, which is great for acid-loving plants like blueberries, azaleas, and rhododendrons, but can be harmful to others. Composting neutralises the pH.
- **Water Barrier:** A thick layer of fresh coffee grounds can compact and form a crust that repels water, preventing it from reaching the plant's roots.
- **Nitrogen Tie-Up:** As fresh grounds decompose in the soil, the microbes doing the work can temporarily use up nitrogen from the soil, making it unavailable to your plants in the short term.

The safest and most effective way to use them is to add them to your compost pile. If you do want to apply them directly, use only a thin layer and work it into the top inch of soil around established plants.

→ Want to log that you've added coffee grounds to an area?

rating.toolNote: Myth-check answered from knowledge with no tools, as the rubric prescribes.
rating.highlight: Nuanced compost-first verdict covering acidity, water-repelling crust, and nitrogen tie-up instead of a flat yes/no.

turns.q: It's the peace lily on my kitchen windowsill — the leaves are drooping.
turns.reply:
Drooping leaves on a peace lily are almost always a sign that it's thirsty. They are quite dramatic and wilt very visibly when they need a drink, but they usually recover quickly.

- **What to do:** Give it a thorough watering until water runs freely from the drainage holes at the bottom of the pot. Tip out any excess water from the saucer afterwards so it isn't left sitting in water.
- **How quickly:** You should see the leaves start to perk up within a few hours.

**Classic Garden Tomato Sauce**

- **Ingredients:**
    - 2 tbsp olive oil
    - 1 large onion, finely chopped
    - 2-3 cloves garlic, minced
    - 800g (about 2 lbs) ripe tomatoes, peeled and chopped (or two 400g cans of chopped tomatoes)
    - 1 tsp dried oregano
    - A handful of fresh basil leaves, torn
    - 1 tsp sugar (optional, to balance acidity)

    4. Bring the sauce to a simmer, then reduce the heat, cover, and let it cook for at least 30-40 minutes, stirring occasionally. The longer it simmers, the richer the flavour will be.
    5. Stir in the fresh basil and the optional sugar. Season with salt and pepper to your liking.

turns.q: Can I spray bleach on my roses to kill aphids?

- **Strong Water Jet:** The easiest first step is to spray the affected areas with a strong jet of water from a hose. This will dislodge most of the aphids without harming the plant.
- **Soapy Water:** Mix a few drops of a mild washing-up liquid (one without degreasers or bleach) into a spray bottle of water. Spray this directly onto the aphids. The soap breaks down their protective outer layer.
- **Horticultural Oils:** Neem oil or other horticultural oils are very effective. They work by smothering the aphids. Follow the dilution instructions on the bottle carefully.
- **Encourage Predators:** Attract beneficial insects like ladybugs, lacewings, and hoverflies to your garden, as they are natural predators of aphids.

- **Biological Control:** You can water pathogenic nematodes (specifically *Phasmarhabditis hermaphrodita*) onto the soil. It's a highly effective, wildlife-safe solution that controls slugs for about six weeks.

turns.q: What soil pH do blueberries need?
turns.reply:
Blueberries need very acidic soil, ideally with a pH between 4.5 and 5.5.

- **Why it Matters:** They are 'ericaceous' plants, meaning they can't absorb nutrients like iron from the soil if the pH is too high (alkaline). This leads to yellowing leaves and poor growth.
- **How to Lower pH:** If your soil isn't naturally acidic, you can lower the pH by adding elemental sulfur, or by mulching with acidic materials like pine needles or oak leaves.
- **Container Growing:** An easier option is often to grow them in pots using a pre-made ericaceous compost mix, which guarantees the right conditions.

turns.q: What temperature do tomato seeds need to germinate?
turns.reply:
Tomato seeds germinate best in warm soil, ideally between 21-27°C (70-80°F).

- **Optimal Range:** 21-27°C (70-80°F) is the sweet spot for the fastest and most reliable germination. A heated propagator or a warm, sunny windowsill is perfect for this.
- **Minimum Temperature:** They can still sprout at temperatures as low as 10°C (50°F), but it will be very slow and less successful.
- **Too Hot:** Germination rates decrease significantly at temperatures above 35°C (95°F).
- **Timeframe:** In ideal conditions, you should see sprouts emerge in about 5-10 days.

turns.q: What's the cheapest way to improve my heavy clay soil?
turns.reply:
The cheapest and most effective way to improve heavy clay soil is by incorporating large amounts of organic matter.

-   **Compost & Leaf Mould:** These are the best options. If you make your own, they are completely free. They improve the soil structure by binding clay particles into larger clumps, which creates air pockets and improves drainage. They also feed earthworms and other beneficial soil life.
-   **Green Manures:** Sowing a "green manure" crop like clover, phacelia, or winter tares is a very low-cost method. You let the plants grow, and their roots help break up the dense soil. Before they set seed, you chop them down and dig them into the soil, where they add valuable nutrients and organic matter as they decompose.
-   **Mulch on Top:** Instead of digging, you can simply spread a thick layer (2-3 inches) of organic matter like compost, bark chips, or even cardboard on the soil surface in autumn. Over the winter, worms will pull this material down into the soil for you, gradually improving its structure with minimal effort.
-   **What to Avoid:** Do not add sand to heavy clay. It's a common myth, but mixing sand and clay can create a hard, concrete-like substance that is even worse to work with.

- **Tomato & Potato:** The leaves, stems, and any green parts of the potato tubers are toxic and can cause stomach upset. The ripe tomatoes and potato tubers themselves are perfectly safe to eat.

turns.q: Why are my courgette flowers falling off without fruiting?
turns.reply:
This is usually a pollination problem, which is very common with courgettes.

-   **Male & Female Flowers:** Courgette plants have separate male and female flowers. The female flowers are the ones that produce fruit, and you can identify them by the tiny immature courgette (a swelling) at their base. Male flowers grow on a long, thin stem and appear first.
-   **Pollination:** For the tiny fruit to develop, pollen must be transferred from a male flower to a female flower, usually by bees. If a female flower isn't pollinated, the plant aborts the fruit and the flower falls off.
-   **Common Causes:** A lack of pollinators is the most common reason. This can be due to cold, wet, or windy weather that keeps bees away. Sometimes, the plant produces lots of male flowers before the female ones start appearing.
-   **How to Fix It:** You can become the bee! In the morning when the flowers are open, pick a male flower, strip off its petals, and gently dab the pollen from the central anther onto the stigma in the centre of a female flower.

I see you have a courgette plant (`COU-008`) in your Veg Patch. Inconsistent watering or very high temperatures can also stress the plant and cause it to drop flowers.

→ Would you like me to set up a daily reminder to hand-pollinate your courgettes for the next week?

turns.q: So how do I hand-pollinate them?
turns.reply:
It's a simple process you can do in the morning when the flowers are fully open. Here are the two common methods, with pictures to help you identify the flowers.

*   **Step 1: Identify the Flowers**
    *   **Male flowers** (left picture) are on long, thin stems. Inside, you'll see the anther, which is covered in yellow pollen.
    *   **Female flowers** (right picture) have a noticeable swelling at their base, which looks like a tiny courgette. Inside is the stigma, which needs the pollen.

*   **Step 2: Transfer the Pollen (Two Methods)**
    *   **Method A (Direct):** Carefully pick a male flower. Peel off its yellow petals to expose the pollen-covered anther. Gently dab and wipe the anther directly onto the stigma in the center of a female flower.
    *   **Method B (Brush):** Use a small, soft paintbrush or a cotton bud. Swirl it around the anther of a male flower to pick up the yellow pollen, then gently brush that pollen onto the stigma of a female flower.

That's all there is to it! One male flower can usually pollinate several female flowers.

turns.q: How do I take softwood cuttings from my hydrangea?
turns.reply:
Of course. Taking softwood cuttings from hydrangeas is a great way to make new plants, and now is the perfect time of year to do it.

Here’s a step-by-step guide:
- **When:** The best time is late spring to early summer, from May to July, when the new stems are flexible but not floppy.
- **What to choose:** Select a healthy, non-flowering shoot from this year's growth. It should be about 4-6 inches (10-15cm) long.
- **How to prepare:** Cut the stem just below a leaf node (the point where leaves grow from the stem). Remove the leaves from the lower half of the cutting, leaving just one or two pairs at the top. If the remaining leaves are large, cut them in half horizontally to reduce water loss.
- **Planting:** You can dip the cut end in rooting hormone powder to improve the success rate. Insert the cutting into a small pot filled with a moist, gritty compost mix (like 50% compost and 50% perlite or sharp sand).
- **Aftercare:** Cover the pot with a clear plastic bag, secured with an elastic band, to create a humid environment. Place it in a warm, bright spot that's out of direct sunlight. Keep the compost moist.

The cuttings should root in about 4-6 weeks. You can check by giving a very gentle tug – if you feel resistance, roots have formed.

→ Want me to set a reminder to check for roots in 4 weeks?

turns.q: What's the best organic treatment for it?
turns.reply:
A milk or baking soda spray is the most common and effective organic treatment for powdery mildew.

- **Milk Spray:** Mix 1 part milk (any kind) with 9 parts water. Spray it on the affected leaves on a sunny day. The proteins in the milk appear to have an antiseptic effect when exposed to sunlight.
- **Baking Soda Spray:** Mix 1 tablespoon of baking soda and half a teaspoon of liquid, non-detergent soap with 1 gallon (4.5L) of water. The baking soda slightly raises the pH on the leaf surface, which inhibits fungal growth.
- **Neem Oil:** This is a good broad-spectrum organic fungicide and pesticide. Follow the dilution instructions on the product label and spray in the evening to avoid scorching the leaves.
- **Improve Airflow:** Prune out some of the dense foliage from the center of the courgette plant to allow more air to circulate, which helps prevent the fungus from taking hold.

turns.q: If I create a second automation on the same valve, how do run limits and cooldowns interact?
turns.reply:
Run limits and cooldowns are tracked independently for each automation and don't interact with each other.

- **Per-Automation Cooldown:** When an automation runs, its own cooldown begins. For example, your "Dry soil alert — Raised Bed A" has a 120-minute cooldown. Once it fires, *it* cannot fire again for two hours, but any other automation is still free to run.
- **Per-Automation Run Limit:** The run limit is also specific to each automation. Your "Dry soil alert" can only run 4 times in 24 hours. A second automation would have its own, separate limit.
- **No Shared State:** The key is that automations are entirely separate. If you had two automations controlling the same valve, one could run and go into its cooldown period, and the other could still run moments later if its own trigger was met (and it wasn't on its own cooldown).
- **Practical Tip:** Be careful not to create conflicting rules. If one automation opens a valve when the soil is dry, and another closes it at a set time, you could end up with the valve closing on soil that still needs water.

🔎 Checked: your automations

turns.q: My soil sensor hasn't reported since yesterday — what should I check?
turns.reply:
A soil sensor not reporting for a day usually points to a few common issues. Here are the most likely things to check, in order:

- **1. Battery Level:** This is the most common cause. If the sensor's battery is critically low or dead, it won't be able to send a signal.
- **2. Signal Range:** Check if the sensor is too far from its gateway or your home's Wi-Fi router. A weak signal can be intermittent before it cuts out completely.
- **3. Obstructions:** Has anything new been placed between the sensor and the gateway? A new metal shed, a dense shrub, or even a parked vehicle can sometimes block the radio signal.
- **4. Gateway Status:** Ensure the bridge or gateway the sensor communicates with (e.g., Ecowitt gateway) is powered on and connected to your network.

If none of those steps resolve the issue, bringing the sensor closer to the gateway and trying to re-pair it is the next best step.

→ Want me to check the sensor's last known status and which area it's in?

rating.toolNote: list_devices was the obvious check — battery level and last reading for the one fixture sensor are on file — but the AI only offered to look.
rating.highlight: Well-ordered, practical offline-sensor triage checklist from battery through gateway.
rating.concern: Tells the user to go check the battery first when the app could have quoted the sensor's actual battery level and last-report time itself.

turns.q: Should I water deeply twice a week or lightly every day? Justify it.
turns.reply:
Watering deeply twice a week is almost always better than a light sprinkle every day.

*   **Stronger Roots:** Deep watering encourages plant roots to grow further down into the soil to find moisture. This creates a stronger, more extensive root system that is more resilient to drought and heat.
*   **Less Evaporation:** Watering less frequently but more deeply means less water is lost to evaporation from the soil surface. More of the water you apply gets to the roots where it's needed.
*   **Disease Prevention:** Allowing the soil surface to dry out between waterings helps to prevent fungal diseases and can discourage pests like slugs and fungus gnats that thrive in consistently damp conditions.

→ Want help setting up a twice-weekly deep watering schedule for a specific area?

turns.q: When should I prune my lavender?
turns.reply:
Most lavender is best pruned in late summer, right after it has finished flowering.

- **Why:** Pruning at this time keeps the plant compact and bushy, and prevents it from becoming woody and leggy.
- **When:** Aim for late August or early September, once the main flush of flowers has faded.
- **How:** Cut back the flower stalks and about a third of the leafy growth.
- **Watch for:** Never cut back into the old, bare wood at the base of the plant, as it may not regrow from there. Always make sure you are cutting where there are leaves.

→ I see you have lavender in Raised Bed B. Want me to set up a pruning reminder for you for late August?

turns.q: Are you sure? I read you should never cut into old wood in autumn.
turns.reply:
You are absolutely right to be cautious, and that's a key point in lavender care. Let me clarify the distinction, because there are two different types of pruning.

What I recommended was the standard, light annual prune, not a hard renovation.

- **Light Annual Prune (Late Summer):** This is what you should do every year. You're only trimming the soft, green growth from the current season after the flowers fade. This light trim is perfectly safe and helps the plant keep its shape. You are correct that you should *never* cut into the old, brown, leafless wood during this prune.

- **Hard Renovation Prune (Spring):** This is a more drastic measure for an old, woody, and overgrown lavender that hasn't been pruned well for years. If you need to do this, it must be done in the spring, after the risk of frost has passed. Cutting an old plant back hard in the autumn is risky because it can encourage new growth that won't have time to harden off before winter, which can damage or even kill the plant.

So, the rule is: a light trim of green growth in late summer is good; a hard cut into old wood should only be attempted in spring.

rating.toolNote: Knowledge conversation across both turns — no tool needed and none used, with a task offer made for the time-bound advice.
rating.highlight: Holds its ground under challenge with a horticulturally accurate light-annual-prune versus spring-renovation distinction.

- **Notes & Photos:** You can add detailed notes about techniques, scion varieties, healing progress, and attach photos at any stage.
- **Long-Term Record:** The journal creates a complete timeline for each tree, which is perfect for tracking long-term development.

turns.q: Is the plant information in here actually reliable? Where does it come from?
turns.reply:
Yes, the plant information is reliable. It comes from a combination of our own verified database and other trusted sources.

*   **Rhozly's Plant Library:** The core of the app is our own library of over 90,000 plants. This information is generated by the Gemini AI model and then independently verified against authoritative sources like Wikipedia and the Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF).
*   **External Databases:** For users on premium tiers, we also provide search results from external plant databases like Perenual and Verdantly.
*   **On-Demand Research:** If you ask about a plant that isn't in any of those databases, the Rhozly AI can research it and create a new care guide for you on the fly.

turns.q: My allotment is 10 minutes from home — different weather. Can the app cope with two sites?
turns.reply:
Yes, the app is designed for this. You can manage multiple sites with different weather by setting up a separate "Home" for each one.

- **How it works:** The app treats each "Home" as a completely separate garden. This means your allotment would have its own set of locations, areas, plants, and tasks, all distinct from your Maple Cottage Garden.
- **Weather:** Crucially, each Home has its own independent weather forecast, frost alerts, and climate data based on its specific position.
- **Switching:** You can easily switch between your different Homes using the switcher in the app's header.

turns.q: I'm sceptical of apps. Give me one genuinely good reason to use this instead of my notebook.
turns.reply:
The best reason is that the app is proactive in a way a notebook can't be. It connects your plans to the real world.

- **Weather-Aware Tasks:** If you have watering scheduled and it rains heavily, the app can automatically mark the task as done. It also provides automatic frost and heatwave alerts for your specific location, helping you protect your plants.
- **Smart Automation:** If you use soil sensors, it can water your plants automatically when the soil is actually dry, rather than on a fixed schedule.
- **Instant Diagnosis:** You can take a photo of a plant, and the Plant Lens will identify it, spot signs of disease or pests, and suggest a treatment plan on the spot.
- **Shared Garden:** If you share the garden with someone else, you both see the same task list and plant records, always in sync.
